course: CMU 21-254: Linear Algebra and Vector Calculus for Engineers
audience: SI leader teaching live on a whiteboard. This is not a student worksheet.
toneRule: Keep explanations very simple, verbal, and board-friendly. Avoid textbook phrasing unless the problem needs it.
title: Exam 2 Review
sessionGoal: Students should review the core Exam 2 skills: inverses, determinants, eigenvalues/eigenvectors, diagonalization, projections, and cross product.
quickStartIfUnprepared: Start by saying: Exam 2 is mostly about what matrices do and what directions they preserve.
Do Problems 2 and 3 if eigenstuff is the priority.
Do Problems 4 and 5 if students want vector geometry review.
Use Problem 1 as a quick warm-up to connect inverses and determinants.
sayThisFirst: For Exam 2, always ask: is this an algebra question, an eigenvalue question, or a geometry question?
Invertibility is about whether a matrix can be undone.
Eigenvectors are directions that do not rotate under the matrix.
Diagonalization is useful because it makes powers easy.
Projection and cross product are geometry tools.
boardPlan: Finish with projection and cross product geometry.
keyFormulas: A matrix is diagonalizable when it has enough independent eigenvectors.
\|a\times b\| = area of the parallelogram spanned by \(a\) and \(b\).
commonMistakes: Assuming a repeated eigenvalue automatically means not diagonalizable.
Mixing up algebraic multiplicity and geometric multiplicity.
Putting eigenvectors as rows instead of columns in P.
Using cross product in 2D without first embedding or using the correct formula.
Forgetting that \(AB\) and \(BA\) may have different behavior.
notationWarnings: Use \(\lambda\) for eigenvalues and \(v\) for eigenvectors.
Use \(PDP^{-1}\) and keep the columns of P matched with the diagonal entries of D.
Use arrows like \(\overrightarrow{AB}\) for vectors between points.

problems.title: Inverse warm-up with a system
problems.whatToSayBefore: Say: determinant first tells us whether the inverse exists.

drawInstructions: Draw the flow b -> A^{-1}b -> x.
Write determinant nonzero means the map can be undone.

walkthrough.label: Check the determinant
walkthrough.latex: \[\det(A)=2(1)-1(1)=1.\] Since the determinant is not zero, \(A^{-1}\) exists.

commonMistakes: Forgetting the determinant in the denominator.
Switching the wrong entries in the inverse formula.
Solving \(bA^{-1}\) instead of \(A^{-1}b\).

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 4 for inverses and determinants.

problems.title: Repeated eigenvalue and diagonalizability
problems.studentPromptLatex: Let \[A=\begin{bmatrix}3&1\\0&3\end{bmatrix}.\] Find the eigenvalue, find its eigenspace, and decide whether \(A\) is diagonalizable.
problems.whatToSayBefore: Say: repeated eigenvalues are not automatically bad. The real question is how many independent eigenvectors we get.

drawInstructions: Draw one invariant direction along the x-axis.
Show that there is only one independent eigenvector direction.
Explain that a 2 by 2 matrix needs two independent eigenvectors to diagonalize.

walkthrough.label: Read the eigenvalue
walkthrough.latex: Since \(A\) is triangular, the eigenvalue is the diagonal entry: \[\lambda=3\] with algebraic multiplicity 2.

walkthrough.label: Find the eigenspace
walkthrough.latex: \[A-3I=\begin{bmatrix}0&1\\0&0\end{bmatrix}.\] Solve \((A-3I)x=0\): \[x_2=0,\quad x_1\text{ is free}.\] So the eigenspace is \[\operatorname{span}\{(1,0)\}.\]

walkthrough.label: Decide diagonalizability
walkthrough.latex: The eigenspace is 1-dimensional, so there is only one independent eigenvector. A \(2\times2\) matrix needs two independent eigenvectors to be diagonalizable.

commonMistakes: Saying repeated eigenvalue means automatically not diagonalizable.
Forgetting to compute the eigenspace.
Thinking algebraic multiplicity and geometric multiplicity are always the same.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 5 for eigenvalues and eigenspaces.

problems.title: Use an eigenbasis to compute \(A^3x\)
problems.studentPromptLatex: Suppose \(v_1=(1,1)\) is an eigenvector with eigenvalue \(4\), and \(v_2=(1,-1)\) is an eigenvector with eigenvalue \(2\). If \(x=(3,1)\), compute \(A^3x\).
problems.whatToSayBefore: Say: if a vector is written in the eigenbasis, powers of A just raise the eigenvalues to that power.

drawInstructions: Draw x as a combination of v1 and v2.
Under A^3, label the new vector as 2(4^3)v1+1(2^3)v2.

walkthrough.label: Apply A^3 to each eigenvector piece

commonMistakes: Multiplying the coordinates by \(4\) and \(2\) instead of \(4^3\) and \(2^3\).
Trying to compute \(A\) first even though the eigenbasis information is enough.
Forgetting to convert back to standard coordinates.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 6 for diagonalization and powers.

problems.title: Projection in R^3
problems.whatToSayBefore: Say: the vector being projected onto controls the denominator.

drawInstructions: Draw u as the direction line.
Draw the perpendicular leftover from the projection tip to v.

walkthrough.label: Subtract to get the perpendicular part

commonMistakes: Using \(v\cdot v\) in the denominator.
Forgetting that \(v_{\perp}\) should be perpendicular to \(u\).
Mixing up \(\operatorname{proj}_{u}(v)\) and \(\operatorname{proj}_{v}(u)\).

problems.title: Cross product, normal vector, and area
problems.studentPromptLatex: Let \(a=(1,2,0)\) and \(b=(3,-1,2)\). Compute \(a\times b\) and the area of the parallelogram spanned by \(a\) and \(b\).
problems.whatToSayBefore: Say: cross product gives the normal vector, and its length gives the parallelogram area.

drawInstructions: Draw a and b starting at the same point.
Draw a normal vector labeled a x b.
Label the area as ||a x b||.

commonMistakes: Forgetting the negative sign on the \(\mathbf{j}\)-component.
Using dot product instead of cross product for area.
Reporting the cross product as the area instead of its magnitude.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 6 for cross product.

wrapUpQuestions: What is the difference between algebraic multiplicity and geometric multiplicity?
Why does diagonalization make powers easier?
When should you use projection vs. cross product?
qualityControlNotes: Exam review problems are original and cover the intended Exam 2 topics.
Checked projection and cross product arithmetic.
reviewSummary: Session 15 is an Exam 2 review set with five mixed problems and visual specs for each major geometry/eigen concept.
